title: CHAPTER 5 AGRICULTURAL CROPS (COMPENSATION) ACT
text:
CHAPTER 5
AGRICULTURAL CROPS (COMPENSATION) ACT
• Act • Subsidiary Legislation •
ACT
Act No.9 of 1967
Amended by
SRO 32 of 1995
ARRANGEMENT OF SECTIONS
1. Short title.
2. Damage to agricultural crops.
3. Disagreement as to amount of compensation.
4. Power to amend Schedule.
Schedule
CHAPTER 5
AGRICULTURAL CROPS (COMPENSATION) ACT
An Act to make provision for the amount of compensation payable for damage
to agricultural crops and other trees.
[Act No. 9 of 1967 amended by SRO 32 of 1995.]
[11th March, 1967.]
1. Short title
This Act may be cited as the Agricultural Crops (Compensation) Act.
2. Damage to agricultural crops
The amount of compensation payable for damage to agricultural crops and other
trees shall be as follows—
(a) where the damage amounts to total destruction, the compensation shall
be calculated at the rates set out in the Schedule;
(b) where the damage does not amount to total destruction, the
compensation shall be calculated on that proportion of the amount for
total destruction directly attributable to the damage as may be
determined by assessors appointed by the parties within four days of the
occurrence of the damage.
3. Disagreement as to amount of compensation
Where the parties or their assessors disagree as to the amount of compensation
payable, either party may make application in writing to the Director of Lands and
Surveys within seven days of the occurrence of the damage to determine the
compensation payable by arbitration, and thereafter the provisions of the Agricultural
Small Tenancies Act, Chapter 10, shall apply, mutatis mutandis.
4. Power to amend Schedule

The Minister, if he or she considers it expedient to do so, may by Order amend the
Schedule.
Schedule

Note.—FOR SUGAR CANE = The values quoted are based on row planting, 5 feet between rows,
and sets of 30 cm (1 foot) long within the rows. The actual rows should be measured, excluding
spaces between rows.
Where cane is interplanted with other crops, those crops should be valued separately.
The economic life of a field is expected to be 6 years (5 crops). After that period, there should be
no compensation if the farmer has to give up the land.

Note.—For forest trees—
1. Where there are two prices for immature trees and plants, the figure in bracket indicates the price for plants raised in
the nursery.
2. Unless otherwise stated in the individual columns: seedlings refer to nursery plants and those planted in the field up
to one (1) year old. Immature trees refer to plants between one year old and 5.5 inches Diameter at Breast Height
(DBH) where DBH is measured at 4 feet 7 inches from the ground. Mature trees are those greater than 5 feet 5 inches
DBH.
3. All mature trees will be cut into logs. The length of the log and its diameter at midpoint will be measured. The volume

of the recoverable lumber is estimated using Scribner’s formula—
V = (0.7D2 - 4) L/16
Where V= Volume D = Mid diameter of log L = Length of log.
4. Figures quoted are maximum. The person assessing the damage may use their discretion, for example, deformed
stems, rotten trunk and conservation impact.
THE ABOVE SCHEDULE WAS PREPARED BY A COMMITTEE COMPRISING REPRESENTATIVES FROM THE MINISTRY OF
AGRICULTURE: AGRONOMY, EXTENSION, FORESTRY AND LANDS AND SURVEYS VISIONS; THE COMMODITY BOARDS:
COCOA, NUTMEG, BANANAS AND COMMERCIAL FARMERS.
CHAPTER 5
AGRICULTURAL CROPS (COMPENSATION) ACT
SUBSIDIARY LEGISLATION
No Subsidiary Legislation
